sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 40, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Character (black L-shape with white dots) starts at (15,18)-(23,26), moves 3 cells per action - Dark gray L-shape at (15,36)-(23,44), moves opposite horizontally, same direction vertically - Yellow L-shape target at (45,51)-(53,59) - likely the goal - Sky blue divider at cols 30-32 - character can pass through - Yellow bar at col 63 shrinks by 1 per action (HUD/timer) - Movement: RIGHT moves char right 3, dark left 3; DOWN moves both down 3
- Plan: Navigate character to overlap the yellow L-shape. Do 11 RIGHTs first (to avoid collision with dark object), then 10 DOWNs.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 39 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After the ACTION7:
- Character moved from (45,51)-(53,59) to (42,51)-(50,59) - moved up 3 rows
- Character children: white dots at (43,52), (43,55), (43,58) and yellow dot at (46,58) and white dot at (49,58)
- Yellow shape is at (45,51)-(53,56) with 36 pixels
The character rotated and moved up. The children show:
- Horizontal row at row 43: (43,52), (43,55), (43,58) - all white
- Vertical column at col 58: (43,58), (46,58), (49,58) - white, yellow, white
This is the same orientation as before: horizontal bar on top, vertical bar on right.
But wait - the result said `gameplay_changed: false` and `no_impact: true`. Yet the character clearly moved from (45,51)-(53,59) to (42,51)-(50,59). This seems contradictory.
Actually, looking more carefully at the result, it says `stop_reason: 'no_impact_action'`. This means the action was flagged as having no impact on gameplay, but the board did change. The system's HUD model might be incorrectly classifying this.
The key observation: the character rotated but ended up in the SAME orientation (horizontal bar on top, vertical bar on right). This means the rotation didn't actually change the orientation - it's cycling through states where some rotations produce the same visual result.
Wait, but the character moved up. So ACTION7 always moves the character up by 3 and rotates. But the rotation seems to cycle: after 4 rotations, we're back to the same orientation. But the current state after 5 total rotations shows the same orientation as after 1 rotation.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the character only has 2 distinct orientations (not 4), alternating between them with each ACTION7. Or maybe the character's orientation is determined by its position relative to something.
Actually, looking at the children more carefully:
- Before this ACTION7 (at step 38): children were Y,Y,Y,W,W at positions (46,52), (46,55), (46,58), (49,58), (52,58)
- After this ACTION7 (at step 40): children are W,W,W,Y,W at positions (43,52), (43,55), (43,58), (46,58), (49,58)
The positions shifted up by 3 (from row 46 to 43, etc.), but the relative arrangement is the same. The colors changed: before had 3 yellow + 2 white, now has 1 yellow + 4 white.
The yellow shape regenerated from 18 pixels to 36 pixels when the character moved away. This confirms the yellow shape "heals" when not overlapped.
So the pattern is:
1. When character overlaps yellow shape, some yellow pixels get absorbed (turn into yellow dots on character)
2. When character moves away, yellow shape regenerates and character's dots revert to white (except maybe some stay yellow?)
3. The character's orientation doesn't seem to change with ACTION7 - it stays the same
Wait, but the first time I rotated (step 26), the character was at (45,51)-(53,59) and moved to (42,51)-(50,59). Let me check what the orientation was before that first rotation.
Actually, I realize I need to reconsider the whole approach. Maybe the goal isn't about orientation matching at all. Maybe the goal is simply to have the character overlap the yellow shape, and the level completes when all yellow pixels are absorbed.
But the character can only absorb the top horizontal bar (3 yellow dots), not the left vertical bar. The remaining 18 pixels of the yellow shape are the left vertical bar.
Maybe I need to try a different approach. What if SPACE does something? Or what if I need to move the character in a specific way to absorb all parts of the yellow shape?
Let me try pressing SPACE to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try SPACE action to see if it does something special
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
